input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Digital Twin implementation leverages Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) frameworks, employing SysML to document functional and logical architecture across abstraction layers (requirements, functional, logical, product/technical). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML Interaction Block Diagrams are used to capture component‑connector relationships and interface definitions. Behavioral modeling is achieved through MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines, with simulation executed in MathWorks Simulink or comparable simulation engines. Code generation targets general‑purpose languages like C++ or Java for runtime deployment. This technology stack is supported by configuration and version control tooling to integrate heterogeneous notations and enable collaboration among diverse stakeholders.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs a model‑based system engineering (MBSE) approach that integrates multiple modeling languages and tools. Computational models are defined in SysML (particularly Integrated Block Diagrams) to describe system components, interfaces, and interconnections, and in MontiArc with its Automaton extension to express component behaviours via state machines. Logical and product/technical layers are captured using the RFLP methodology (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) or OOSEM/SPES for higher‑level abstraction. Simulation models are provided in MathWorks Simulink, while executable models or behavioural logic can be implemented in general‑purpose programming languages such as C++ or Java. Data sources for the twin are implicit in the physical objects being shadowed or mirrored; data is collected via the digital shadow reference model (Fig. 1) which aggregates contextual data traces and abstractions for a specific purpose, creating a continuous one‑way data flow into the twin for monitoring and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: An automated one‑way data flow between the state of a physical object and a digital object, as defined in the digital shadow reference model. The document does not specify the data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data quality assurance, preprocessing steps, or data integration mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin’s communication stack is built on Mosquitto, the open‑source MQTT broker described in document 52, which implements the MQTT 3.1.1 protocol with explicit support for TLS encryption (currently TLS 1.2/1.3) and username/password authentication; optional client‑side certificates can be used for mutual TLS to guarantee that only authenticated controllers exchange telemetry.  At the application level, the platform employs Thingsboard (document 53) as an IoT back‑end, providing a multi‑tenant architecture with fine‑grained role‑based access control, JWT‑based device authentication, and mandatory TLS for all MQTT, HTTP and WebSocket traffic, with integrated audit logging for device, tenant and subscription events.  In the overall architecture, the RAMI 4.0 reference model (document 48) offers a dedicated cybersecurity layer that mandates risk assessment procedures, fail‑safe safeguards and a comprehensive resource‑based access control system, ensuring that safety‑critical data flows are isolated, encrypted, and monitored.  The Asset Administration Shell (document 50) encapsulates security metadata at the asset level—security categories, access rules, and cryptographic keys—allowing automated enforcement of ownership and authentication policies when the twin interfaces with the physical robot.  Finally, the functional mockup interface (FMI, document 51) can be employed to exchange simulation models in a version‑controlled, signed‑file format, further protecting model integrity and preventing unauthorized modification.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
